title: Origami water cup
subtitle: Folding a functional drinking cup from a single square of paper
excerpt: The origami water cup is one of the classic functional folds: made from a single square, it holds water briefly and is one of the most direct demonstrations of origami's practical origins. The fold sequence is short enough to memorise after two repetitions, and the geometric logic behind the cup shape illustrates the basic principles of diagonal folding that run through most origami models.
sourceNotes: The origami water cup (masu-gata) is a traditional Japanese fold documented across numerous origami teaching sources in the public domain. The fold sequence described here is consistent with the standard teaching version.

glossaryTerms.definition: A fold where the paper bends toward you, creating a valley between the two faces. The fold line sits at the bottom of a V-shape when viewed from the side. The most common origami fold direction.
A fold where the paper bends away from you, creating a ridge. The fold line sits at the top of a mountain shape when viewed from the side. The reverse of the valley fold.

content.text: The water cup is made from a single square of paper and follows eight steps. Use a 15 cm kami sheet for practice. For a cup that actually holds water, use a larger sheet, 20 cm minimum, of less absorbent paper (copy paper or washi holds water longer than kami).

the square in half diagonally to produce a triangle, coloured side out. Crease firmly. Step 2: With the long edge of the triangle facing you, fold the bottom right corner up to touch the left edge of the triangle. The fold line runs from the bottom-left corner. Crease. Step 3: Fold the bottom-left corner up to touch the right edge. Crease. The model is a diamond shape with two triangular flaps at the top. Step 4: Fold the front top triangular flap down over the front of the model. Step 5: Turn the model over. The fold you just made becomes a

from this new face. Step 6: Fold the other top triangular flap down over the back. Step 7: Open the top by pressing the two outer points toward each other, the cup opens. Step 8: Press the base flat to make a stable standing cup.

The two side folds in steps 2 and 3 create the angled walls of the cup. They divide the triangle into thirds along the long base; if the fold is not at exactly one-third from each end, the cup will be asymmetric and the sides will not be even in height. The geometric relationship is: the angle of each side fold equals the arctangent of 0.5, which is about 26.6 degrees. Getting this fold accurate by eye takes a few attempts.

The cup holds its shape best when every crease is pressed firmly to its full length. A bone folder gives sharper creases than a fingernail. When working with a 15 cm kami sheet, the model is small enough that a fingernail works, with larger sheets, use the bone folder. Accurate alignment at each step matters more than the sharpness of the crease: a precisely folded cup with soft creases works better than a sharply creased cup with misaligned folds.
